input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware hierarchy comprises a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and autonomy elements (e.g., sensors, actuators, and ECU). The platform operates as a testbed for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) where on‑board ECUs are resource‑constrained, making it a suitable candidate for off‑loading computation to a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three concrete services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) displays real‑time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks; it also allows users to view, save, and generate reports of historic state data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation) is executed on the DT (cloud) instead of the vehicle, with the resulting desired speed transmitted back for actuation. (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set a target speed, and issue an emergency‑braking command through the UI, allowing full remote control of the ACC function. Target users are operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting via data collection).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) as the middleware for message passing between the vehicle and the Digital Twin, and on NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the software development kit that provides GPU‑accelerated compute and driver support. Application code is written in Python 3.8 and C++ for real‑time components, while MATLAB/Simulink is used for algorithm prototyping and model‑based design. The DT also incorporates low‑latency communication links, data collection and reporting modules, and a safety‑critical UI layer to satisfy the stringent timing and reliability requirements of the ACC system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The twin requires a shared notion of time across devices; synchronization accuracy of 100 ms is mandated (hardware‑limited) and complies with safety‑critical vehicular standards that often demand tighter (<100 ms) sync. This 100 ms bound governs the coordination needed for computation off‑loading between the physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based hierarchy with three distinct instances: a Physical Twin Entity, a Digital Twin Entity, and a User Entity. Each entity is deployed on a separate hardware component, yielding a decentralized, multi‑entity architecture where responsibilities are split—Physical Twin handles sensing, actuation and low‑level processing, Digital Twin manages data management and control logic, and User Entity provides visualization and control panels.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration is built on the ISO 23247 reference architecture; software components are mapped to Functional Entities and each FE is packaged as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. Communication and coordination among the three entities occur via ROS 2 middleware, allowing distributed execution across heterogeneous compute resources.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability follows ISO 23247 part 2 and may employ standards such as OPC Unified Architecture for data exchange, although the present implementation uses ROS 2 topics/services. The design therefore supports integration with external enterprise systems or other digital twins through OPC UA or ROS 2 bridges, aligning with the standard’s information‑exchange guidelines.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates several computational models. A point‑cloud perception pipeline performs geometric clustering with the DBSCAN algorithm on LiDAR data and behavioural tracking with a Kalman filter, forming the \'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking\' model. An Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) model consumes vehicle‑state data (speed, odometry) to generate longitudinal control commands, representing an analytical behavioural model. The data sources feeding these models are the Hokuyo UST‑10LX 2‑D LiDAR, the Intel Deepsense D435i depth camera, and on‑board vehicle‑state sensors (wheel encoders, IMU). Sensor streams are published on ROS 2 topics and consumed by the respective model nodes. Processed outputs and raw measurements are persisted by a dedicated Data Storage component, while a Report Generation component formats runtime statistics for later analysis. Model relationships are explicit: raw sensor data → perception models → control (ACC) → actuation commands, and all data are managed within the ROS 2 middleware framework.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The architecture requires that all devices share a common notion of time with a synchronization accuracy of 100 ms, as stated: many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization than 100 ms for safety‑critical vehicular applications, but the system limits to 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data originating from the physical twin—including LiDAR point clouds, motor status, steering‑servo positions, and vehicle state information—is streamed to the Digital Twin System (DTS) over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. The raw LiDAR points are first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which converts the unstructured point cloud into clustered object tracks. All incoming data streams are then passed to the Data Translation functional entity (FE) that serialises the data, aligns message types, and performs required unit conversions. Within the DTS the processed data flows between software components via a ROS2 (DDS) middleware network. The resulting ACC‑relevant data (vehicle state, object tracks, projected driving path) is rendered in real‑time on the 3D Visualization UI.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the digital twin are issued to the physical twin through the Actuation functional entity (FE). The ACC Controlling FE computes a desired speed based on lead‑vehicle detection and sends a speed command, while remote‑management functions (enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, emergency‑brake) are also transmitted. Commands are serialized by the Data Translation FE and transmitted over the same Wi‑Fi UDP channel used for sensor data. Upon receipt, the Actuation FE drives the motor and steering‑servo to realise the commanded actions. Explicit acknowledgement or confirmation of command execution is not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS employs a two‑layer network architecture. Internally, software components exchange messages on a ROS2 (DDS) network, providing publish‑subscribe communication within the digital environment. Communication between the physical twin and the digital twin occurs over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. The system is required to support low end‑to‑end latency to satisfy safety‑critical ACC operation, although specific latency or bandwidth figures are not provided. No explicit reliability, redundancy, or security protocols are described in the supplied material.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: ACC computation is offloaded from the vehicle’s resource‑constrained ECU to the Digital Twin, which can run on cloud resources as indicated by the statement that computation is allocated to 'better‑suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud.' No further details on the hosting platform, computational or storage capacity, scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability guarantees are given in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Key software components are first identified (Table I) and their relationships to sensors and actuators are defined (Fig. 2). Each component is then mapped to a Functional Entity (FE) and implemented as an individual ROS 2 package; a package may contain one or more ROS 2 nodes that realise the FE’s task. The mapping organises the software according to the ISO 23247 entities: the Digital Twin Entity hosts data‑management and control components, the Physical Twin Entity hosts data pre‑processing, actuation and generic automotive functions (e.g., point‑cloud clustering & tracking), and the User Entity contains the User Interface FE split into a 3‑D visualisation and a control‑panel component. Each entity is deployed on a distinct hardware component of the vehicle, reflecting the standard’s entity‑based distribution. The process therefore consists of: (1) identification of SW components, (2) FE mapping, (3) ROS 2 package implementation, and (4) deployment onto the vehicle hardware.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system is built on the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing. Specifically, Part 2 of ISO 23247, which defines the entity‑based reference architecture (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) is used as the primary design basis. The implementation follows the ISO‑defined entities but introduces a Physical Twin Entity that extends the standard’s OME concept. Functional Entities are instantiated as ROS 2 packages, aligning with the standard’s allowance for arbitrary FE implementation (e.g., OPC UA, ROS 2). The design acknowledges deviations from the ISO model and notes their impact on ownership and engineering processes.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the nature and configuration of the physical twin described in the text. It correctly identifies the scale, retrofitting details, hardware hierarchy, and the specific motivation (resource-constrained ECUs) for using a digital twin. Quotes: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th]-scale autonomous vehicle', 'physical architecture consists of three layers... lower level chassis... upper level chassis... and the autonomy elements', 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction precisely lists the three services defined in the case study and their specific functionalities. It also correctly identifies the target users (operators and developers) and their roles. Quotes: 'Data Monitoring... UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports of vehicle state data', 'Computation Offloading... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'Remote Management... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed', 'users of our system can be grouped into two classes: operators and developers'.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific software tools (ROS 2, Jetpack 5.0, Python, C++, MATLAB/Simulink) and their roles, along with the necessary communication and data collection requirements mentioned in the source. Supported by quotes: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Python 3.8... C++... and MATLAB/Simulink'.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the 100 ms synchronization requirement and its justification found in the source. The text states: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and explains this is 'due to hardware limitations' while noting stricter standards exist for safety-critical applications. It also links this to computation offloading: 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

The extraction correctly identifies the three distinct entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) described in the Architecture section. It accurately notes their separation onto different hardware components: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'. The responsibilities assigned to each entity (e.g., Physical Twin handles sensing and actuation, Digital Twin manages data) align with the SW component descriptions in Table I and Section IV-B.

The extraction correctly describes the orchestration based on ISO 23247 and the packaging of Functional Entities as ROS2 packages. The source confirms: 'we map the SW components onto FEs and implement them... implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes'. The role of ROS2 middleware in communication is also supported by the text.

The extraction accurately references ISO 23247 part 2 and the potential use of OPC UA as a standard for information exchange. The source states: 'Part 2 of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA... The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented. For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA)'. It correctly identifies that the implementation uses ROS2 topics/services. The mention of alignment with information-exchange guidelines is supported by the context of the standard's purpose, though specific bridge implementations are not detailed in the snippets.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework

and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control

the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

It correctly identifies the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' model's use of 'DBSCAN' and a 'Kalman filter' and the 'ACC' model's role, directly from Table I. It also correctly identifies the 'Data Storage' (SQL database) and 'Report Generation' components. However, it includes unsupported hardware details for vehicle-state sensors ('wheel encoders, IMU'), as the source only mentions 'Vehicle State' as a software component without specifying the hardware sensors.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly describe physical acting components. The text mentions a 'lower level chassis' responsible for steering and speed control, a 'Motor', 'Steering Servo', and a 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller'.

desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
